flashcards.front: What is a process in operating system context?
flashcards.back: A process is an instance of a program in execution. It is a fundamental unit of work in an operating system, consisting of the program code, current activity, and associated resources like memory, CPU registers, and I/O devices.

PCB is a data structure maintained by the operating system for each process. It contains process state, program counter, CPU registers, memory management information, accounting data, and I/O status information.

flashcards.front: List the key components of a process.
flashcards.back: The key components are: (1) Program code (text section), (2) Program counter, (3) CPU registers, (4) Stack (temporary data), (5) Data section (global variables), and (6) Heap (dynamically allocated memory).

flashcards.front: What is the difference between a program and a process?
flashcards.back: A program is a passive file stored on disk containing instructions, while a process is an active entity created when the program is loaded into memory and begins execution. Multiple processes can run the same program simultaneously.

flashcards.front: Name the five basic process states.
flashcards.back: The five basic process states are: NEW (created but not yet admitted), READY (waiting in queue for CPU), RUNNING (currently executing on CPU), BLOCKED/WAITING (waiting for I/O or event), and TERMINATED (finished execution).

Process scheduling is the mechanism by which the operating system decides which process should occupy the CPU at any given time. It involves managing the ready queue and selecting the next process to run based on scheduling algorithms.

Context switching is the process of saving the state of a currently running process and restoring the state of another process. It involves storing and loading registers, updating the PCB, and switching memory addresses.

A child process is a process created by another process (parent process) through a system call like fork() in Unix/Linux. The child process inherits various attributes from its parent but gets a unique PID.

A zombie process occurs when a child terminates but its parent has not yet read its exit status. An orphan process occurs when a parent terminates before its child; the init process becomes its new parent.

flashcards.front: What is multi-tasking in operating systems?
flashcards.back: Multi-tasking is the ability of an OS to execute multiple processes concurrently by rapidly switching the CPU between them. It creates the illusion of parallelism despite having a single or limited number of CPUs.

A CPU-I/O burst cycle describes the alternating periods when a process executes CPU instructions (CPU burst) and waits for I/O operations (I/O burst). Processes typically alternate between these two types of bursts.

flashcards.front: What is the role of the dispatcher in process scheduling?
flashcards.back: The dispatcher is the component that gives control of the CPU to the process selected by the short-term scheduler. It handles context switching, switches to user mode, and jumps to the proper location in the selected program.

flashcards.front: What is the difference between long-term and short-term schedulers?
flashcards.back: Long-term scheduler (admission scheduler) selects which processes enter the ready queue from the new state, controlling the degree of multiprogramming. Short-term scheduler (CPU scheduler) selects which ready process gets the CPU next.

flashcards.front: What is an interrupt in process management context?
flashcards.back: An interrupt is a signal to the CPU indicating an event that needs immediate attention. It can be caused by hardware (I/O completion) or software (system calls), causing the OS to pause the current process and handle the event.

Degree of multiprogramming refers to the number of processes that are currently in memory and actively competing for CPU time. Higher degrees increase CPU utilization but may reduce average response time.
